lines.text: Section seven. The RNA world — how life might have started.
We have been talking about minimal cells and synthetic cells from the standpoint of subtractive engineering — taking an existing cell and reducing it.
The final two sections of this chapter zoom out to ask a much older question: how did life arise in the first place?
And could we recapitulate that emergence in the laboratory?
The dominant contemporary theory is that life began with a self-replicating molecule, or perhaps an assembly of self-replicating molecules.
The earliest replicators were not cells — cells came later — but simply molecular systems capable of making copies of themselves from environmental building blocks.
And the leading candidate for what those early replicators looked like is some form of nucleic acid, very much like the RNA we know today.
Why nucleic acids? The most compelling reason is complementarity.
Recall from chapter one that the complementarity of base pairing in DNA is what allows one strand to serve as a template for the next.
If you have a molecule that can pair with itself, you have an automatic template-copying mechanism — and template-copying is exactly what you need for replication.
Hence the suggestion that nucleic acids, or nucleic-acid-like molecules, were the first self-replicators.
There are alternatives.
Some researchers favour self-replicating peptides — short chains of amino acids that catalyse their own synthesis from amino acid precursors.
Others propose self-organising lipid systems as the seed of life, with metabolism arising first and replication second.
These alternatives may have emerged in parallel with nucleic acids, or perhaps preceded them, and the field is genuinely uncertain about the exact sequence.
But the case for an early RNA-based world is supported by several lines of evidence in modern biology.
Modern cells are full of molecules that are clearly descended from an RNA-dominant past.
ATP and other cofactors are essentially modified nucleotides.
The ribosome — the machine that synthesises every protein — is at its catalytic core a ribozyme, made of RNA.
Many other RNA molecules with catalytic activity are known.
All of this points to an earlier era — the RNA world — in which RNA molecules both stored information (in their sequences) and performed catalysis (through their structure).
Modern cells delegate storage to DNA and catalysis to proteins, but RNA's continued central role in the most ancient cellular processes is a kind of fossil from that older world.
Researchers have not just speculated about the RNA world; they have run experiments that recapitulate small pieces of it.
One of the earliest extracellular evolution experiments was conducted by the Spiegelman group.
They worked with the bacteriophage Q-beta, which uses an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase — an enzyme that copies RNA from an RNA template — to replicate its own RNA genome.
Spiegelman showed that you could take this enzyme, plus an RNA template, plus a pool of nucleotides — and the system would replicate itself in a test tube, autonomously, with autocatalytic kinetics.
More interestingly, by running the reaction long enough and providing limited resources, Spiegelman showed that variant RNA templates could compete with each other for those resources.

Templates that replicated faster — because of mutations that happened to give them better polymerase affinity, or shorter length, or better folding — outcompeted slower variants, and the population evolved toward faster-replicating sequences.
This was Darwinian evolution, in vitro, in a tube with three components.
The Spiegelman experiments are foundational because they demonstrated that the engine of evolution — variation, replication, differential survival — can run outside any cell.

If you can recapitulate evolution in vitro, you can in principle study what the earliest evolving systems on Earth might have looked like, and you can engineer them in the laboratory to evolve toward useful properties.
Both threads — origin-of-life research and directed evolution for biotechnology — trace back to those experiments.
And the broader RNA world hypothesis remains the dominant framework for thinking about how life began.
